adipose tissue: The image shows adipose tissue, recognizable by its large, circular cells that appear empty or clear.
In the photo, fat cells are visible, each with a thin rim of cytoplasm and a flattened nucleus pushed to the side.
This tissue has a characteristic honeycomb or chicken-wire appearance due to the close packing of adipocytes.
This is a photo of adipose cells, which are distinguished by their large, ovoid shape and lack of internal staining.
The image displays lobules of fat, where large, empty-looking cells are grouped together.
The most prominent feature of the adipose tissue in this image is the large, single lipid droplet that occupies most of the cell volume.
The tissue sample contains adipocytes, which are large, white, and spherical structures under magnification.
This photo shows mature fat tissue, identified by its large, univacuolar cells arranged in clusters.
The scan highlights an area of adipose tissue, which appears as a collection of well-defined, non-staining circular profiles.
Visible in the image is fatty tissue, composed of large cells where the nucleus is barely visible at the cell's edge.
complex stroma: The image displays a complex stroma, characterized by a dense and disorganized mixture of cells and fibrous tissue.
Visible in the photo is a desmoplastic or reactive stroma, with abundant spindle-shaped fibroblasts and thick collagen bundles.
This photo shows a highly cellular stroma, indicating a significant reaction, often associated with an invasive tumor.
The complex stroma in the image contains numerous small blood vessels and a prominent inflammatory cell infiltrate.
This tissue is identified as complex stroma due to its dense, pink-staining, fibrous appearance that separates and surrounds other tissue elements.
The scan highlights a disordered stromal environment with a high density of cells and extracellular matrix components.
In the photo, the stroma is complex and appears actively involved in a pathological process, unlike simple supportive tissue.
This is an image of a sclerotic stroma, a type of complex stroma that is densely packed with collagen.
The tissue shows a complex stromal reaction, with haphazardly arranged cells and fibers.
A key feature of the complex stroma in this image is the lack of the loose, delicate structure seen in normal connective tissue.
debris: The image shows an area of debris, appearing as an unstructured, granular, and intensely pink-staining region.
Visible in the photo is necrotic debris, where cell outlines are lost, leaving behind only amorphous material and cellular fragments.
This is a photo of acellular debris, characterized by the absence of intact nuclei and recognizable cell shapes.
The scan highlights a region of tumor necrosis, which looks like a messy collection of 'ghost' cells and nuclear dust (karyorrhexis).
The tissue sample contains inflammatory debris, composed of dead cells, fibrin, and remnants of neutrophils.
In this image, the debris appears as a collection of irregular, eosinophilic (pink) material without a clear structure.
This photo captures cellular debris resulting from cell death (apoptosis or necrosis).
The area of debris in the image is notable for its lack of viable, staining nuclei.
The scan shows clumps of proteinaceous debris and remnants of broken cells.
This is an image of keratin debris, often seen as laminated, pink flakes in certain types of tumors.
empty background: The image contains an area of empty background, which appears as a clear, white space where no tissue is present.
This photo includes a region corresponding to the empty glass slide, seen as a uniform and featureless background.
A portion of the image shows an empty lumen, which is the clear, open space inside a glandular or ductal structure.
The background of the slide is visible in this photo, appearing as a bright, evenly illuminated area devoid of any cells.
The photo shows the edge of the tissue section against a blank, empty background.
An artifactual space or crack in the tissue is visible, appearing as a clear, empty line in the image.
This is a photo of a region without any biological material, representing the empty background of the microscope slide.
The scan includes a large, featureless area that is the out-of-focus background of the slide.
An empty space is present in the image, likely due to tissue processing or the natural structure of the organ.
The image shows a section of clear, empty space used for slide calibration or as a reference point.
immune cells: The image shows a dense infiltrate of immune cells, appearing as numerous small, round, and dark-staining nuclei.
Visible in the photo are lymphocytes, which are characterized by their scant cytoplasm and large, hyperchromatic (dark purple) nuclei.
This is a photo of a chronic inflammatory infiltrate, composed primarily of small, dark immune cells like lymphocytes and plasma cells.
The stromal tissue in the image is heavily populated by immune cells, seen as a diffuse collection of 'small blue dots'.
The scan highlights clusters of lymphocytes, which are small, round cells with very little visible cytoplasm.
In this photo, plasma cells are identifiable within the immune infiltrate by their eccentric 'clock-face' nuclei and more abundant cytoplasm.
The image displays a band-like infiltrate of immune cells at the interface between epithelium and stroma.
This is an image showing perivascular cuffing, where immune cells are clustered around a blood vessel.
The photo captures a lymphoid aggregate, which is a well-organized, dense collection of immune cells.
Neutrophils are visible in the image as immune cells with multi-lobed nuclei, indicating an acute inflammatory response.
normal mucosal glands: The image displays normal mucosal glands, which are well-organized, circular, or tubular structures lined by a single layer of cells.
In this photo, the glands show an orderly 'back-to-back' arrangement with uniform, basally located nuclei.
Normal glands are visible, appearing as rounded structures with open central lumens and minimal intervening stroma.
This is a photo of healthy mucosal tissue, characterized by its regular and well-differentiated glandular architecture.
The epithelial cells lining the normal glands in the scan have a uniform size and shape, without nuclear atypia.
The image shows glands with clear cell polarity, where nuclei are at the base and mucin-filled cytoplasm is at the top.
This photo depicts regularly spaced crypts, which are the normal glandular structures in the intestinal mucosa.
The scan shows a preserved glandular architecture, a key feature of normal, non-cancerous mucosal tissue.
In the image, the normal glands are uniform in size and shape from one to the next.
A key feature of the normal glands in this photo is the absence of disorganized growth, crowding, or multiple cell layers.
simple stroma: The image shows simple stroma, which is a loose and sparsely cellular connective tissue that supports epithelial structures.
Visible in the photo is a paucicellular stroma, composed mainly of delicate, pink-staining collagen fibers and a few scattered fibroblasts.
This is a photo of normal supportive tissue, appearing as a loose, fibrous network located between glands.
The stroma in this scan is simple and unremarkable, lacking the dense, reactive features of complex or desmoplastic stroma.
The tissue is supported by a simple stroma, which appears as a loose and sometimes edematous (swollen) connective framework.
This image displays simple stroma with thin, wavy collagen fibers and occasional spindle-shaped cells.
The photo shows a delicate, hypocellular stroma that serves as the normal framework for the organ.
In this scan, the simple stroma contains small, inconspicuous blood vessels and very few inflammatory cells.
This is an image of the lamina propria, a type of simple stroma found beneath mucosal epithelium.
The stroma appears simple and quiescent, consisting of a fine fibrillar matrix with widely spaced cells.
tumour epithelium: The image shows tumour epithelium, characterized by disorganized, fused, or back-to-back glands with irregular shapes.
Visible in the photo are malignant epithelial cells with large, dark (hyperchromatic), and variably shaped (pleomorphic) nuclei.
This is a photo of a carcinoma, where the tumour epithelium forms solid sheets and invasive nests within the stroma.
The tumour cells in the scan show a high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio and prominent, often red, nucleoli.
In this image, the normal glandular architecture is lost and replaced by a chaotic proliferation of atypical tumour epithelium.
The photo shows cribriform structures, where tumour epithelium forms sieve-like or punched-out glandular spaces.
A key feature of the tumour epithelium is the loss of cell polarity, with nuclei arranged haphazardly instead of at the base.
The scan highlights tumour cells with significant nuclear atypia, including irregular nuclear membranes and coarse chromatin.
Numerous mitotic figures, some of which are abnormal in shape, are visible within the tumour epithelium.
This is an image of infiltrating tumour epithelium, seen as single cells or small, irregular clusters invading the surrounding stroma.
